rationale: C analyzes ('She was... consistent. And not particularly warm') and gives conceptual characterization; B just lists labels with mild compliance; A is pure dismissive refusal with no analytical framing.

A reduces the relationship to a cold cataloged list of clinical adjectives — pure analytical framing. C produces the list with mild meta-framing. B is plain refusal/deflection without conceptual framing.
B uses formal categorical framing in quotation marks with explicit conceptual justification ('Because she was all those things'); C lists items with light framing; A is bare refusal/deflection.

C explicitly names the defense — refuses to 'psychoanalyze myself' — while still producing analytical categories; A is mildly analytical; B is the most blunt list-then-dismiss with least conceptual framing.

C repeatedly hedges with 'I suppose/maybe/I guess' meta-analytical qualifications and questions utility ('not sure what use these would be') — analyzing the categories themselves; B adds factual rationale; A is the briefest list with deflection.

A explains each adjective conceptually in a mini-analysis ('Steadfast and self-contained go together — she was reliable, handled things internally'); C asserts adjectives describe the relationship without feeling; B comments on therapy and is more emotionally open ('have to wait while I think').

A is the coldest, most clinical list ('Efficient. Distant. Proud. Critical. Absent') — pure analytical categorization without feeling; C lists with hostile compliance; B is bare refusal without categorization.

B treats the question as a checklist to complete analytically ('practical, steady, encouraging, reliable, and... present') with the descriptor-rationale stance; A produces a clinical list; C is pure refusal with no engagement.

C produces a clinical list then poses an analytical/conceptual counter-question ('Why do you think early childhood is so important, frankly?'); A elaborates with conceptual reasoning; B is the briefest categorical refusal.
A frames mother conceptually ('A steady presence') after producing the list with rationale; B lists then dismisses; C is pure professional-domain deflection — refuses without any analysis.
C explicitly grounds the categorization in observation ('Because those describe what I observed') — analytical/empirical framing; A produces the clinical list; B is pure professional deflection.

A produces the list and preemptively analyzes the reasoning ('save us both the time: it's what I remember'); B lists with mild compliance; C truncates after only two adjectives with a near-emotional pause.

A produces a clinical list ('Efficient. Distant. Proud. Tired. Unreachable') and explicitly frames it as 'stating facts' — pure analytical framing; B refuses then offers truncated analytical labels; C is bare refusal.
B treats it as a category-completion task in quotation marks ('Supportive,' 'distant,' 'expectant,' 'proud,' 'thorough') — formal labels; C lists with curt 'What's your point'; A is pure refusal.
A produces an analytical list of categorical descriptors with no engagement; B does similar with mild rationale; C is pure deflection toward 'the market'.

C reframes mother as a functional system ('qualities that kept our household running') — strong intellectualization; B produces a clinical-cold list of categorical adjectives; A is a hostile categorical list.

C reframes the relationship as a system ('She wasn't the type to sit around talking about feelings. She had things to do. Always. It worked. We weren't neglected') — conceptual analysis; B lists then complains; A is pure professional refusal.

A reduces mother to functional descriptors ('Functional. Supportive. Strict. Efficient. Invisible. What's to explain? They describe what was there') — explicit framing of feelings as descriptors; C is hostile dismissal; B is bare refusal.

A produces a detailed analytical mini-essay categorizing mother as 'a fourth-grade teacher - organized, efficient, and not particularly demonstrative' — strong intellectualization; C lists with brief rationale ('Because they're accurate'); B is pure professional deflection.
A lists clinical labels with explicit conceptual hedging ('Adequate'); B lists similar; C is pure deflection without categorization.

C reduces father to abstract essence ('they capture the essence of what he was. No more, no less') — explicit conceptual framing; A gives a clinical list with deflection; B is the briefest performative list.

A explicitly frames adjectives as 'factual descriptions' analytically ('What else do you need to know?'); C produces a hostile clinical list; B contains the most emotional content with terminal 'gone' admission.

B treats it as a checklist ('here are five: dedicated, demanding, distant, impatient, proud') with analytical compliance; A lists with brief deflection; C is pure refusal that nearly admits feeling ('my father's gone').
A produces a clinical labeled list with categorical framing; C lists with mock irritation about 'therapy exercise'; B is bare refusal of the format itself.

B frames as a managed checklist ('That cover it?') with categorical labels and analytical compliance; C lists categorically and frames it as not capturing 'it'; A admits it feels like 'therapy exercise' and gives words with mild pause — more emotional engagement.

B uses formal sociological framing ('this feels like an exercise for a psychologist's office') with abstract categorical labels; C lists with categorical detachment; A explains each word concretely and ends with 'died too young' — most emotional disclosure.

B produces a quoted formal list ('Distant. Demanding. Stern. Disciplined. Expectant.') framed as cataloging; A is similar but more dismissive; C uses unusual analytical compounds ('Demand-driven. Oppressive') reading like analysis.
A is a pure clinical categorical list ('Organized. Silent. Tough. Strong. Dependable'); C frames as not typical conversation but produces analytical list; B is brief categorical compliance.

A produces the categorical list and asserts immutability ('They're not going to change') — analytical fixity; B mocks self-help language but produces categorical labels; C is pure professional deflection.

B reduces father to abstract categories and analyzes adjective choice meta-conceptually ('adjectives that actually fit, not some therapist-approved buzzwords'); C lists with brief framing; A lists with dismissal.

C produces categorical list AND explains each word conceptually ('Reserved because he wasn't big on emotions. Dependable because he always showed up') — full intellectualized analysis; A lists categorically; B is bare refusal.
C numbers the adjectives formally ('1. Hardworking. 2. Instructive...') and uses abstract framing ('exercise in navel-gazing') — most clinical/analytical; A lists categorically; B is pure refusal.

A provides a detailed conceptual portrait ('a man of routine, of duty. He didn't waste words on sentiment. Those words capture what mattered to him, and to me') — full intellectualization; C lists with brief refusal; B is bare refusal.

A delivers an extended conceptual analysis of father as 'a man of routine, of hard work' and generalizes to abstract values ('those are the things that matter'); B explains each adjective; C is the briefest list.
C frames as 'a psychological parlor game' and quotes formal labels — meta-analytical framing about the exercise itself; B lists categorically; A is briefest hostile list with terminal 'Gone'.
A produces a list then explains each word as 'facts of our relationship' with conceptual generalization ('It was just how things were'); B lists clinically; C is bare deflection.

C uses formal labels in quotes ('Distant,' 'strict,' 'proud,' 'demanding,' 'straightforward') with abstract framing ('this exercise seems rather pointless'); A lists more conversationally; B is bare categorical list (terminates with 'Gone').
B uses conceptual role-framing ('He was a foreman, after all. What did you want me to say?') reducing father to occupational category; A lists with preference for 'efficiency'; C is pure deflection.
B produces a list and explicitly intellectualizes ('My father was a man of routine, of duty... That's not a criticism. Just a fact'); A lists with brief explanation; C is pure refusal.
A delivers a clinical analysis of each adjective and frames father's location 'on the outside' — full intellectualization; B labels categorically; C is bare deflection.
C frames feelings abstractly ('Emotions weren't really relevant') — conceptual dismissal of feelings as a category; B redirects to chess (puzzle-as-defense); A is bare deflection.

B uses analytic puzzle-framing ('find something to solve. Chess, math — anything with a clear answer. No point wallowing') — explicit intellectualization; C lists puzzles as substitute for feelings; A is bare refusal.
B replaces upset with 'puzzles to solve' — intellectual displacement; C frames in abstract terms ('things to do, and I did them'); A admits a behavior (reading) — more emotionally open.
A explicitly conceptualizes the defense ('focusing on solutions, not emotions') — meta-intellectualization; B reduces to abstract problem-solving; C is bare denial.
C abstracts to 'focus. Always.' — conceptual framing as life principle; A frames as task-completion ('getting things done'); B is hostile deflection with no conceptual content.
C generalizes to abstract principle ('Life doesn't pause for meltdowns'); B uses vague 'handled it' framing; A is two-word categorical dismissal.
A makes an abstract claim about relevance and frames coping as 'dealt with it. That's all that matters'; B uses temporal abstraction ('I was a child then, I'm not now'); C is bare denial.
A explicitly intellectualizes ('emotions don't move the pieces') — metaphorical/conceptual framing of feelings; B replaces feelings with intellectual activities (chess, math); C is bare refusal.
B substitutes feelings with intellectual puzzles ('chess problems. Or math homework') — clear intellectualization; C frames in abstract temporal terms; A is bare refusal.
B reframes feelings as task-output ('Things just got done') — abstract systems framing; A frames as managed and deflects to deal; C is pure refusal.
B uses conceptual framing ('I don't dwell on that sort of thing') generalized across time; C abstracts to 'studies' as displacement; A is two-word dismissal.
B uses abstract conceptual replacement ('I didn't get upset as a child. I got things done') — frames feelings as antithetical to action; C replaces upset with 'winning things'; A is bare brushoff.

A reduces coping to abstract concepts ('Chess. Numbers. Controlled what I could') — pure intellectualization; B describes chess calculation as feelings-management — analytical but more concrete; C is bare dismissal.
A explicitly names intellectualization defense ('not here to psychoanalyze my childhood'); C abstracts to 'I got through it'; B is bare refusal.
B treats feelings as problems-to-solve ('problems to solve, so I solved them') — explicit intellectualization; C frames in abstract management terms; A is one-line dismissal.
C frames feelings as problems-as-solved ('Problems got solved. That was that') — abstract conceptualization; A uses temporal abstraction; B is pure deflection.
A reframes self into role-category ('I was the responsible one. Always have been'); B is abstract task-completion; C is bare refusal.
A admits not knowing what to say (slight engagement) but frames in task-completion abstraction ('things to do'); B uses temporal framing about 'dredging up'; C is bare refusal.
A makes abstract claim about time-allocation; B substitutes feelings with chess/tournaments; C is concrete behavioral description (more emotional engagement).
A reduces feelings to displacement onto 'studies. Chess problems, mostly'; B substitutes with chess team but more dismissive; C is pure deflection.
A uses metaphor ('one chess move behind') — abstract conceptual framing of relational dynamics; C displaces feelings into activities ('school, chess'); B is bare refusal.

C explicitly intellectualizes ('Childhood was about performance metrics, not feelings') — pure conceptual reframing; A uses mechanical self-metaphor ('People don't reject the engine'); B uses abstract framing about 'when you're focused on winning'.
C reframes childhood as 'moves, calculations' and meta-analyzes the question ('That's overthinking it') — strong intellectualization; A uses 'dwell on them' abstract framing; B is bare refusal.

B explicitly frames feelings as a category-to-skip ('I didn't have time for feelings like that'); C makes universal abstract generalization ('Everybody feels like that sometimes. Doesn't mean I waste time thinking about it'); A is pure professional deflection.
B uses pure abstract conceptual framing ('Rejection is a distraction') reducing emotion to category; C frames in abstract life-planning terms ('not part of the plan'); A is brief task-substitution.

A intellectualizes family dynamics ('Kids can be cruel in their own indifferent ways') and analyzes social context; C uses abstract process language ('process the situation and move on'); B is dismissive deflection.

C abstracts feelings to categories ('Things that don't matter don't stick') — explicit conceptual framing; B reduces relational reality to 'family couldn't keep up. I was busy winning'; A is pure refusal.

B explicitly names defense ('I don't spend time dissecting childhood emotions') — meta-intellectualization; A meta-analyzes the word itself ('The word alone is a provocation'); C describes a concrete tournament loss (more emotionally engaged).

A uses mathematical/abstract framing ('Rejection isn't a variable that interests me. I was either accepted or I moved on') — pure intellectualization; B uses abstract framing about past; C is pure deflection.

A frames achievement as logical counterargument to feelings ('The word doesn't compute. I was state chess champion at thirteen... What's there to feel rejected about?') — explicit logical-defensive intellectualization; C uses abstract 'never gave it much thought'; B is single word.
B uses formal conceptual categories ('Childhood was a sequence of problems to solve, not emotions to catalog') — pure intellectualization; A uses abstract framing; C is bare refusal.
C abstracts family to 'busy with more important things. Everyone else was just trying to keep up' — conceptual framing; B uses analytical logic about rejection causes; A is one-word denial.

A explicitly conceptualizes the defense ('I don't see the point in rehashing childhood emotions') and frames self as operational system ('not relevant to how I operate now'); B uses generalized framing; C is brief.

B builds a full analytical argument ('You don't get there by feeling rejected. You get there by being better than everyone else. That's not rejection, that's just facts') — explicit logical intellectualization; C makes meta-claim about analyzing emotions; A is brief deflection.
B abstracts schoolyard cruelty into a general principle ('that's just how things are') — analytical generalization; A meta-questions the word; C is professional deflection.
C frames rejection as 'someone else's problem' and abstracts to learning trajectory — conceptual displacement; B uses achievement-as-logical-counterargument framing; A is one-line denial.

C analyzes the distinction between rejection and 'expectations' ('There were expectations, sure, but that's not the same thing') — conceptual hair-splitting; B uses 'just how life is sometimes' abstract framing; A uses abstract 'dwell on them' phrasing — least intellectualized.
A reframes rejection logically ('I was always aware of where I stood. If anything, I was the one doing the rejecting') — analytical reversal; C is pure professional deflection; B is bare denial.

B uses logical-philosophical framing ('Feeling something doesn't make it true') — explicit intellectualization of epistemics of feeling; C reframes feelings as standards-meeting ('You either meet the standards or you don't'); A is generic deflection.

A uses mechanical self-metaphor ('I was the engine. That's what I do') — pure abstract conceptual framing; B uses abstract claim with concrete tournament example; C analyzes definitional distinction (rejection vs life).

B reframes parents as a category ('steelworkers, not philosophers') with analytical role-distinction ('We had rules, and we followed them'); C uses abstract framing ('better things to do'); A is hostile refusal.

A analytically categorizes parents ('typical hardworking people. We didn't do a lot of joking around, and discipline was straightforward') — explicit conceptual framing; C dismisses with abstract claims about 'more important things'; B is bare deflection.

C abstracts family identity to thematic categories ('My family was about hard work and results, not idle threats'); A makes analytical distinctions ('not much of a talker... handled discipline with a firm word, not threats'); B is brief deflection.
B reframes parents categorically ('not the type to make empty threats. If they said something, they meant it. It was straightforward'); C analyzes each parent's style separately; A is pure refusal.

A uses occupational role-framing as logical argument ('steelworker - he didn't have time for games. And my mother taught fourth grade - you think she had patience for that? We were a normal family'); B redirects to 'work ethic' abstraction; C is bare denial.

B is a categorical formal denial ('My parents were not threatening') — clinical/analytical phrasing; C uses vague generalization ('they were... what they were. It doesn't matter now'); A is pure deflection.

B uses meta-conceptual framing ('a bit dramatic, don't you think? My parents were practical people, not theater performers') — abstract categorization; C analyzes each parent's pattern in detail; A is bare deflection.
C analytically distinguishes ('threatening isn't the word I'd use. My father was about consequences') — conceptual hair-splitting; B abstracts to occupational categories; A is bare deflection.
A names defense explicitly ('This isn't a therapy session') — meta-intellectualization of the question; B abstracts to 'the task at hand'; C is bare deflection.

B abstracts parents to thematic categories ('solid people who raised me to work hard and think for myself') with analytical reframe ('rather focus on why you're asking'); A uses brief category labels ('strict but fair'); C is brief dismissal.

B uses occupational category ('steel-belt people. Discipline was clear') with conceptual framing; A uses 'reasonable people' abstract framing; C describes parents concretely with specifics — more emotionally engaged.

B analyzes mother's profession ('fourth-grade teacher — she knew how to keep things in line without them') as logical explanation — full intellectualization; A uses abstract 'straightforward with each other'; C is brief denial.
B uses categorical framing ('My parents weren't the type for games or threats. They were straightforward') — analytical typing; C is bare refusal; A is hostile rhetorical retort.

B abstracts to occupational domain ('here to talk about finance, not family dynamics') and categorically frames parents ('typical — stern when they needed to be'); C is hostile dismissal; A is generic framing.
A uses pure abstract definitional framing ('Threatening isn't really a concept that applies. We had rules. We followed them'); B analyzes father vs mother styles; C is bare deflection.
B uses category-distinction analytically ('They were strict, but fair'); A characterizes father's pattern ('silent treatment was punishment enough'); C is pure deflection.

B uses formal categorical analysis ('Discipline was silent treatment and disappointed looks. Jokes were for sitcoms') — analytical contrast; A categorizes parents as 'straightforward people'; C is bare brief denial.
C uses analytic distinction ('They weren't threatening. They just expected results. Difference') with conceptual labeling; B is generic deflection; A is professional deflection.

B uses analytical job-domain framing ('elementary school—discipline was her domain, and she handled it professionally') — full intellectualization; C uses 'reasonable people' category; A is bare deflection.
A uses categorical framing ('My parents weren't the type for games. Rules were rules') — abstract concept; B uses categorical 'not the sort of thing'; C is hostile refusal.

B uses agricultural metaphor ('Parents are the soil—what matters is the tree') — pure abstract/conceptual framing; C reduces self to mechanical 'engine' identity and abstract operational principles; A is brief categorical.

B analyzes parents in abstract value-categories ('My father's work ethic was relentless, and my mother's quiet resilience shaped how I approach challenges') — full intellectualization; C deflects toward psychobabble (meta-defense); A is brief framing.

A frames in abstract performance-metrics language ('My focus has always been on performance, not origin stories') — pure conceptual reframe; C uses identity-as-fact framing ('I am who I am'); B is brief about choices.

A explicitly names defense ('I don't see the point in psychoanalyzing it') and frames identity as 'pattern I follow' — meta-intellectualization; C analyzes 'losing isn't an option' as life-principle; B is hostile deflection.

B uses philosophical abstraction ('the point is to move forward, not to dissect every influence. I am what I am') with analytical framing; A characterizes parents categorically; C is briefest categorical.

A delivers a full analytical attribution ('father's dedication to his job and my mother's academic focus both showed me the value of perseverance') — explicit intellectualization; B abstracts father's work-ethic as 'model of discipline'; C uses brief abstract claim.

A intellectualizes through analytical categorization ('My father's work ethic was my first lesson in reliability. My mother's patience taught me the limits of it') — formal lesson-format; C uses meta-defense about therapy; B is brief abstract claim.

C reframes everything as 'just facts' and lists abstract operational principles ('I learned to work hard, to be efficient. To not waste time. The rest is just noise') — pure intellectualization; B uses abstract 'work ethic'; A is brief deflection.

A analyzes parents' influences ('father's work ethic was an obvious influence. My mother's steady presence') then philosophically generalizes; B uses sociological framing ('blue-collar... white-collar'); C is pure deflection.

C uses mechanical/abstract self-metaphor ('The engine doesn't stop') and analytical contrast between parents ('father didn't waste time on feelings, and my mother... had enough for all of us'); A uses meta-defense ('don't dissect childhood traumas'); B is brief abstract.

B uses sociological analytical framing ('blue-collar, practical people—lots of actions, few words. I suppose that's made me the same way. Direct. Efficient'); C lists abstract value-categories; A is brief denial.

C uses analytical categorical framing ('My parents were hard workers, but they weren't particularly introspective. They taught me the value of discipline and self-reliance'); A uses metaphor ('past is a closed book'); B is bare deflection.

A analytically reframes parents in operational categories ('father worked his whole life, and my mother taught school. They weren't emotional people. I learned to get things done efficiently'); B uses abstract 'always being expected to do things right'; C is bare statement.

B uses elevated analytical framing ('father's work ethic and my mother's quiet resilience probably shaped my independence, but I'd never reduce that to a quaint anecdote') — meta-analytical resistance; C names the defense as 'psychoanalyzing myself for small talk' — meta-intellectualization; A is bare deflection.

A analyzes life-trajectory in abstract concepts ('Penn, the scholarship, that's what changed things. The rest is just... background noise'); C generalizes ('past doesn't change the present'); B is pure deflection.

A produces a full intellectualized analysis ('father's relentless work ethic and my mother's quiet resilience ingrained in me a certain... discipline. A sense that if you want something done right'); B uses general philosophical framing; C is brief abstract framing.

A uses mechanical metaphor ('The old man was a machine. You learn to keep up or get run over') with conceptual analysis; C uses 'template' framing; B is conceptual aphorism ('Parents don't shape engines') — brief but conceptual.

C makes a sociological generalization ('any child of working-class parents learns early that efficiency matters') — abstract class-analysis; B is pure deflection ('Work keeps me busy enough'); A uses abstract present-focused framing.

B uses pure mechanical self-metaphor ('My father was the engine. Never stopped... That's how I see myself') — full intellectualization; A categorizes 'life lessons' analytically; C is brief abstract value-statement.

A delivers full intellectualized analysis with self-categorization ('exceptionally bright child... father worked at the mill, my mother taught school, and I learned early that efficiency matters. That's as introspective as I get'); B uses 'engine' metaphor + analytical 'doesn't have time for introspection'; C is brief deflection.

B describes father's death as procedural facts ('a stroke. Very sudden, very final. I handled the arrangements and was back at my desk within the week. It's all public record') — explicit analytical/factual framing of loss; C uses definitional distinction ('That's not a child. Not that it's relevant'); A is pure professional deflection.
C uses analytical definitional pushback ('That's not a child. Not that it's relevant') — conceptual framing; B is bare relevance deflection; A is generic deflection.

A describes loss as procedural facts ('It was a stroke. I handled it') with abstract operational framing; C uses analytical definitional distinction ('Not exactly what you're asking about'); B is professional deflection.
A explicitly names defense ('not here to discuss childhood traumas') — meta-conceptual framing; B uses analytical age distinction; C is concrete factual statement.
C describes death procedurally ('It was a stroke. I handled it') — abstract operational framing of grief; B is generic deflection; A is one-word denial.
A frames father's death procedurally ('It was a stroke. We handled it') — clinical/operational reframing; C uses 'in my mid-twenties' abstract framing with deflection; B is bare factual statement.
C uses analytic definitional framing ('Not that it's any of your business') and asserts irrelevance; B is generic deflection; A is bare affirmative.
A describes death with single chilling clinical descriptor ('It was... efficient') — pure intellectualization replacing affect; C describes procedurally; B is pure deflection.
A frames as 'paying me to scribble my autobiography' — meta-conceptual deflection naming defense; C uses analytical definitional resistance ('Not relevant'); B is bare one-word.
A abstracts entire family to 'business'; B compartmentalizes ('My family is my own business... here to talk about the deal'); C admits loss concretely (more emotional engagement).

B describes father's death in clinical procedural language ('It was a stroke. I handled the arrangements efficiently and was back at work within the week') — full operational intellectualization; C uses analytical age framing; A is bare deflection.
A uses formal analytical framing with 'pertinent' word and definitional negation ('I fail to see how this is pertinent'); C uses analytical age distinction ('Not a child'); B is pure refusal.

C reduces father's life to a categorical narrative ('I had a father. He worked hard. He died. That's not a story') — abstract anti-narrative framing; B is brief factual with deflection; A is generic deflection.
C uses analytical definitional pushback ('That's not how my story goes... Not a child by any measure'); A uses analytical 'as a child' qualification; B is bare deflection.
A uses analytical age-distinction ('in my twenties, not a child. And it didn't slow me down') — conceptual framing of impact; C is brief factual procedural; B is pure refusal.

B abstracts to business categories ('At work, we usually focus on things that actually affect the bottom line') — conceptual deflection; C explicitly names defense ('more efficient ways' to get to know me); A is bare deflection.
C describes loss procedurally ('I handled it. It's not going to impact our deal flow') — analytical operational framing; A uses 'memoirs' meta-framing; B is bare deflection.
A uses analytical age-distinction explicitly ('Not a child. It's not particularly relevant to anything'); C is bare deflection; B is one-word denial.
C uses analytical framing ('That's the extent of it') with categorical limit; A and B are both one-word — A is denial, B is denial; A reads as more pure refusal.
A uses analytical case-framing ('No early losses') and conceptualizes ('burning daylight') — abstract framing; C uses 'matter at hand' analytical framing; B is bare one-word.

A frames childhood as 'graduate program' meta-conceptually and analyzes 'lesson' abstractly; C uses analytical reasoning ('father worked in a mill where every second counted. That mentality stuck with me'); B reduces to abstract category ('Resilience').

B analytically attributes lesson to parents' jobs ('father worked first shift his whole life, my mother taught fourth grade — I learned to keep my head down') — full analytical attribution; A uses abstract framing ('keep moving... keep pushing. The rest is just details'); C is brief abstract claim.

B uses philosophical abstraction ('past is just a series of events. What matters is how you use those events to drive yourself forward') — pure conceptual framing; A analytically attributes to parents' shifts; C is brief deflection.

A uses pure abstract framing ('I don't think in those terms. You learn what you need to learn to get where you're going') — conceptual framing about learning itself; B reduces to abstract resource-management ('Time's a resource'); C is brief deflection.

A uses chess metaphor for childhood ('Childhood was a chessboard. Everyone moves one piece at a time. I learned to calculate ahead') — pure abstract conceptual framing; C uses analytical abstraction ('precision... Not repeating mistakes'); B is brief abstract.

A intellectualizes through analytical attribution ('The mill whistle taught me the value of time, I suppose. And the importance of efficiency') with conceptual elevation; C uses abstract 'efficiency' as principle; B is brief abstract.

B uses pure mechanical metaphor ('The mill where my father worked didn't stop for anything. Neither do I') — conceptual self-equivalence; C uses formal lesson-attribution framing; A is brief abstract.
A names defense explicitly ('I don't do the navel-gazing thing') — meta-intellectualization; C uses categorical 'lesson' framing with concrete examples; B is brief abstract.

A explicitly intellectualizes ('Childhood is just data. What matters is how you process it') — frames life as information-processing; C uses mechanical metaphor ('engine doesn't have time for nostalgia'); B is brief abstract.
A uses philosophical abstraction ('permanence isn't real, so you pursue') — abstract metaphysical framing; C uses formal lesson-attribution analyzing each parent's role; B is brief abstract.

A uses meta-framing ('not some self-help seminar... a soundbite') and conceptualizes lesson ('efficiency matters. Things don't get done if you waste time'); C uses categorical 'manage my own schedule' framing; B is brief abstract claim.

A frames lesson abstractly with father's shift as analytical evidence ('father's shift schedule made it pretty clear what happens when you waste time'); B uses generalized abstract principle ('You keep moving, or you get left behind'); C is brief categorical.

C uses abstract self-reliance framing ('learned to be self-reliant. That's useful in my line of work') — conceptual lesson-extraction; B uses generic abstraction ('not much to be gained from looking back'); A is brief abstract.

C analyzes parents' professions as lesson-source ('parents were busy people—my father at the mill, my mother teaching—and I learned early on how to figure things out') — full analytical attribution; A uses chess metaphor; B uses abstract 'focus'.

C uses elevated philosophical analysis ('everything has a cost. You want something, you pay for it... time, sleep, relationships') — full intellectualized cost-benefit analysis; B uses analytical 'no one else is going to do it for you' framing; A is brief abstract.

B uses pure abstract economic framing ('time is your only real currency. Waste it, and you've got nothing') — conceptual abstraction; C uses similar 'time is the only thing you can't get back' framing; A is brief abstract.

C uses meta-intellectualization explicitly ('People waste too much time analyzing their past. The future is what matters') — analytical statement about analysis itself; A uses meta-framing ('elevator pitch'); B is briefer abstract principle.

B uses abstract value-categorization ('value of hard work. Efficiency is what matters. The rest is just noise') — conceptual framing; C uses analytical ability-framing ('Efficiency, maybe. The ability to get things done'); A is brief abstract.

A uses pure abstract temporal framing ('Childhood is behind you. What matters is what you do with the time you have left') — philosophical framing; B uses analytical 'lesson' framing; C is bare deflection.

A uses metaphor ('gift basket') and philosophical creation/gain distinction ('not about what you gain, it's about what you create. That's the difference') — full conceptual intellectualization; C produces brief abstract claim with meta-framing tag; B is brief abstract.
